talk_title: Population-based cancer surveillance informs disparities and policy

speaker.name: Dr. Ahmedin Jemal
speaker.affiliation: Senior Vice President of Surveillance, Prevention, and Health Services Research, American Cancer Society, Atlanta, GA

models.name: US Cancer Statistics (ACS Cancer Facts & Figures) annual reports
models.genotype_or_key_features: nationwide incidence, mortality, survival by age, sex, race, geography

clinical_data.population: US cancer patients overall (2023 Cancer Stats article)

MOA_summary: Dr. Jemal presented integrated analyses of national cancer registry and survey data revealing sex-specific lung cancer incidence reversals, early-onset colorectal cancer birth-cohort effects, and the impact of ACA Medicaid expansion on insurance coverage, stage at diagnosis, and five-year survival, linking surveillance metrics to policy outcomes.
key_findings: Lung cancer incidence in women under age 50 now exceeds that of men, a reversal not explained by smoking prevalence alone.

Birth-cohort analysis shows a fourfold increase in rectal cancer risk and twofold increase in colon cancer risk for cohorts born after 1950, prompting USPSTF to lower colorectal screening start age to 45.
Geographic clusters with 40% higher colorectal cancer mortality identified in the Lower Mississippi Corridor, leading to targeted screening initiatives and legislative action.
Medicaid expansion under the ACA reduced uninsured rates more than private-market subsidies and improved five-year overall survival in high-poverty rural areas.
Dependent coverage expansion increased early-stage diagnosis and fertility-sparing treatment rates for cervical cancer in women aged 21‑25.
open_questions: How can cancer registry data be integrated with genomic and treatment data to better understand rural disparities?
What methods can reduce the lag time in treatment data reporting to inform real-time policy decisions?
